pages.name: Arthen Moll — Deputy Voice of Civic Reconciliation

text.content:
Triggered the afternoon of the morning evidence review, or first thing the following morning if the party stays out late. Trigger Arthen Moll's visit when the party has identified at least two of the three names (Cindren & Vhal, Gilst, Moraven) but before they have committed to a travel approach.

Who he is

Arthen Moll, Deputy Voice of Civic Reconciliation. Mid-fifties. Round, well-dressed in pearl-gray and dark blue. Speaks with a polished, friendly smile and the gentle authority of a man whose job is to make conflicts look smaller than they are.

The Office of Civic Reconciliation is a Yeomanry administrative body created originally to mediate trade disputes between guilds. Under the current Spokesman it has expanded — quietly — into a softer cousin of the Council's public-closure faction (Phase 1). Moll is its most visible deputy. He is not personally corrupt. He is, however, convinced that drawing attention to financial irregularities damages the Yeomanry's reputation more than it protects its institutions, and he acts on that conviction.

The conspirators do not own Moll. They do not need to. They benefit from his sincere belief that some things are best handled quietly.

How he arrives

By appointment, with two clerks carrying small leather portfolios. He is announced at the Little Palace's front gate by a Yeomanry footman who hands a card to Elsbet, who walks it up to whichever room the party is occupying. The card reads:

Arthen Moll · Office of Civic Reconciliation
A brief courtesy call. Half an hour, no more, with the gratitude of the Yeomanry.

If the party refuses to receive him, he waits politely in the Reception Parlor (M2) for forty minutes, leaves a sealed letter with Elsbet, and goes home. The letter contains the same content as the speech below, slightly more formal, with two passes of underlining.

Where he is received

Moll expects the Reception Parlor (M2). Caelith deliberately suggests the Library (M5) instead — denser, less ceremonial, harder for Moll to perform in. Moll accepts gracefully and the meeting moves there.

Trina, if she is in the Little Palace at the time, slips into the Library a moment after Moll arrives and takes the chair at the writing desk by the window. She does not speak unless directly addressed.

Read-aloud — Moll's pitch

Moll smiles when he sees you, the same smile he gave the footman, the same smile he will give the cook on the way out. He does not sit until the party sits.

"Friends of the Common Defense," he says, with feeling. "What you have already done for the Yeomanry will be remembered for at least one full council session. I will not flatter you with more than that. I have come on a small errand from the Office of Civic Reconciliation, which I think you will find a sensible one."

"The matter you are presently engaged with — the bonded firms, the unfortunate clerk, the missing factor on Tannerway — is, as you have no doubt seen, a matter of account-keeping irregularity. The Yeomanry has institutions for that. The Treasury, the Audit Hall, the Bureau of Crop Estimation. They are slow, but they are ours, and they have been resolving such matters since long before any of us were born."

"The Office of Civic Reconciliation would like to express, formally and gratefully, that you have done enough. We would like you to consider a small honorarium — twelve hundred gold each, paid by Yeomanry draft, not Council coin — and a quiet departure to wherever you are most needed elsewhere. The matter you have surfaced will be pursued by the institutions whose business it is. You will be named in the durable record as the friends who lit the candle. We will, with our slower hands, carry the candle to its lamp."

He looks pleasantly at each of you in turn.

"If you accept, we can have the drafts at this house by sundown. If you do not — and I want to say this clearly, because I respect you — we will not block your inquiry. The Office of Civic Reconciliation does not have that power, and would not use it if we did. We will, however, ask you to consider that visible inquiry by friends-of-the-Yeomanry into the affairs of an Aerdi-licensed firm has consequences for our trade. The eastern banks watch. They will read your visibility as policy, and we will pay the cost in basis points for two quarters."

"That is all. I will not press you. May I leave the drafts here, in case you change your mind tonight?"

What he is actually saying

  • The bribe is real. 1,200 gp each, drawn on the Yeomanry's reconciliation fund. The money is clean; the office is real; the offer is sincere on its face.
  • The threat is institutional, not violent. Moll cannot stop the party. He can, however, and will, file a formal note with the Council that the party's continued visible inquiry is making the Yeomanry's eastern trade more expensive. Council members of his faction will then begin asking Caelith uncomfortable questions in private session, which costs Caelith political capital.
  • He believes himself. He is not lying. He has worked the trade-irregularity beat for fifteen years and his sincere position is that loud strangers always make things worse.

Insight checks

  • Insight DC 12: Moll is sincere about wanting them to take the money and stop. He does not enjoy delivering this errand but he believes it is the right one.
  • Insight DC 15: the line about "the eastern banks watch" is not Moll's own framing — it is something he has been told. He is repeating someone else's argument as his own.
  • Insight DC 18: Moll has had this exact conversation with at least three other parties this season, on three different matters, and the script does not change. He is the wall the soft pressure faction puts in front of inquiries before they get too loud.

If the party accepts

Drafts arrive at sundown by Yeomanry courier. The Pressure Clock does not advance from this scene; the campaign, however, proceeds without the party as the active investigators. Caelith continues quietly. Phase 3 may resume with the party as informants rather than agents. (This branch is supported but not the recommended path.)

If the party declines

Moll smiles, takes back the drafts, and leaves the door open behind him. Caelith, on hearing the refusal, is visibly relieved but does not say so.

Pressure Clock advances by 1 segment. Within 24 hours, two things happen:

  • A formal note from the Office of Civic Reconciliation is filed with the Council, observing that "the foreign-named persons in residence at the Little Palace continue an inquiry of mercantile sensitivity." This costs Caelith one favor.
  • Cindren & Vhal — through Hesren Vesh's earlier sealed message — receives confirmation that the party will not be administratively contained. They escalate. Merev Sarth's retrieval attempt is triggered the following night. See Merev Sarth — Retrieval Attempt.

If the party defers ("we'll think about it")

Moll leaves the drafts "in case you change your mind tonight" and returns the next morning. Pressure Clock advances by 1 segment regardless. Merev Sarth's retrieval attempt is triggered the night the party makes their decision.

How Trina reads the room

If the party asks Trina afterward what she made of him, she sets her cup down and says, very quietly: "He believes what he says. That is the worst kind of obstacle. You cannot persuade him; you can only outwork him."

Triggered on the night following the party's decision to continue (or defer) the inquiry — i.e. the night Cindren & Vhal Hardby receives Hesren Vesh's sealed report and concludes that administrative pressure has failed.

Setup

The conspirators have hired Merev Sarth (see Phase 2 NPCs compendium for full statblock) to retrieve or destroy the contents of the Strong Room. Specifically: the letter of credit (Phase 1 evidence #4), the lacquered scroll tube and its contents (Phase 2 evidence #4–6), and Gilst's bank instrument if the party has brought it back to the Little Palace.

Sarth is on a retrieval contract, not a kill contract. She has been paid more than usual not to leave bodies. She will fight only as a last resort, and she will run the moment retrieval becomes impossible. Her fallback is to douse and ignite the evidence rather than allow the party to keep it.

Her plan

  • Enter the Little Palace via the courtyard service alley (M8 south gate) at the third bell after midnight, dressed in a real Yeomanry courier tabard with a forged Audit Hall inspection writ.
  • Bypass the militia guard with the writ and her courier persona; if the writ is questioned, distract with smoke pellet and flee.
  • Down the spiral stair (M12 → C1) to the cellar.
  • Pick the Strong Room (C5) double lock — Sleight of Hand DC 18 against each lock, with her Stealth and Investigation skills. She has prepared the second lock's tumbler from a wax cast Hesren Vesh's clerk took during a recent Yeomanry inspection.
  • Empty the chest into her courier wallet. Light the chest's contents only if she cannot exit clean.
  • Exit through the Old Service Tunnel (C7) rather than back up to the courtyard. The tunnel is her actual entry/exit; the courtyard approach is misdirection.

What she does not know

  • That Caelith and Trina anticipated this attempt the moment Hesren Vesh's courier left Wick Lane. The contents of the chest are decoys — careful copies of the most diagnostic items, with the originals moved to Trina's townhouse before sundown.
  • That the Old Service Tunnel has been quietly observed for the last three days by Caelith's contact in the Audit Hall (a person Caelith will not name even now); a Yeomanry watch corporal has been positioned at the tunnel's far end since morning.
  • That a low-grade alarm rune has been placed on the inside of the Strong Room door (Caelith's own work; Arcana DC 14 for any PC inside to recognize as such). The rune triggers a soft chime audible in the upper hall (U1) and Caelith's Civic Office (U2).

How the scene plays

Option A — The party is awake or alerted

If the party is awake (e.g., the Warlock or another night-active PC is studying in the Library, or someone heard a sound), they may intercept Sarth at any point along her route. The encounter is short and resolution-focused. Sarth's priority is exit, not victory.

  • If detected before reaching C5, she throws a smoke pellet, uses Cunning Action to Disengage, and flees down the alley toward the tunnel. Stealth opposed by passive Perception (typical 15-17 for an alert party).
  • If detected at the Strong Room door, she yields the lock-pick approach, throws a smoke pellet, and attempts to exit through C7. The Yeomanry corporal at the far end of the tunnel will detain her if she reaches him.
  • If cornered, she fights only long enough to break line of sight; she does not press attacks once she has space to flee.

Option B — The party is asleep, alarm rune triggers

The chime wakes Caelith and any PC sleeping in U2's adjoining suite or U1 hallway (Wisdom save DC 8 to wake at the soft sound; Caelith and Trina are always alert). Caelith and Trina move to the cellar carrying lanterns. The party arrives in time to see Sarth either at the Strong Room door (mid-pick) or already inside, transferring evidence.

Caelith does not draw a weapon. He says, "You will leave that wallet, and you will go through the tunnel as you intended, and the corporal at the other end will explain what happens next." Trina remains silent. Sarth — recognizing two more dangerous people than her client warned her of — drops the wallet and runs for the tunnel.

If the party intervenes here, see Option A combat resolution.

Option C — Sarth gets clean to the tunnel

If for some reason Sarth reaches the Old Service Tunnel undetected and unintercepted, she runs straight into the Yeomanry corporal at the far end. The corporal — a steady Veteran-statted Yeomanry sergeant named Brell Kostan — disarms her and walks her to the Audit Hall under the cover of pre-dawn dark. The party learns of the capture at breakfast.

Combat in the Strong Room (if it comes to it)

Sarth's stats: CR 4, AC 15, HP 65, DEX 17, Sneak Attack +14 (4d6). See actor sheet.

Tactical notes:

  • The Strong Room is a 10×12 ft chamber with a single door. Tight quarters. Sarth's mobility is limited.
  • She uses smoke pellets to obscure the door, not the room. Her goal is to exit, not entrench.
  • She has Uncanny Dodge and Evasion; she is hard to drop quickly. Tier-2 melee will need 2-3 rounds.
  • If reduced to 30 HP or below, she yields. If the party offers her a chance to surrender she takes it. If they do not, she fights to break line and runs.

If captured alive

Sarth says nothing useful for two days. On the third day, if held in Yeomanry custody and treated lawfully, she yields:

  • The job was paid in three drafts of 200 gp each, drawn on different banking houses (none Cindren & Vhal — the laundering layer).
  • The intermediary who hired her is a Hardby contact she knows only as "Tarsh." She will give a description: a thin man in his fifties, with a voice like a smoker's. She does not know his real name and has reason to think he doesn't either.
  • She will not name her broker — the person who connects her to clients in the first place — under any circumstance, including her own life. "The man who hired me has paid in advance for my silence. I respect that."

Caelith offers Sarth a one-time deal: a one-way Yeomanry passport to anywhere in the Sheldomar Valley plus 100 gp, in exchange for testifying once if needed. Persuasion DC 14: she accepts. She is not a fanatic; she is a contractor.

If killed

Caelith is briefly disappointed but absorbs it; her body is searched and the wallet, oilcloth, smoke pellets, glass oil vial, forged writ, and tabard are recovered. The forged writ is itself useful evidence — its counterfeit Audit Hall seal matches a stamp Caelith had been quietly looking for in the Audit Hall's records for nine months. The seal will, when it is finally traced, lead to a specific clerk in Caelith's own office. Phase 2 does not name that clerk.

What the party leaves the night with

  • The originals of all key evidence (always safe; they were at Trina's townhouse all along).
  • One captured (or killed) thief; one forged Audit Hall seal; one description of a Hardby intermediary called "Tarsh."
  • The certain knowledge that the conspiracy can reach into the Little Palace's cellar and is willing to do so. The political pressure (Moll) and the criminal pressure (Sarth) are coordinated, even if the coordinator is not in either room.
  • Pressure Clock advances by 1 segment regardless of outcome — the conspiracy now knows the party is unmoved by either approach and will escalate further if Phase 2 continues.
